title: JTBD Analysis: GfS Türwächter IoT — General Warehousing and Storage (NAICS 493110)

sections.title: Phase 1 — Industry Landscape
sections.content:
### 1.1 CFJ, Output Types, Offering Level

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| NAICS | 493110 |
| CFJ | Receive, store, and dispatch client goods with full inventory integrity |
| Offering Level | L6 — service platform (warehousing is a multi-step operations service, not a discrete product) |

**Output Types:**

| Output Type | Offering Level |
|---|---|
| Ambient Pallet Storage & Distribution | L6 |
| High-Bay Racked Storage | L6 |
| E-commerce Fulfillment (unit pick) | L6 |
| Cross-docking | L6 |
| Value-Added Warehousing (kitting/labeling) | L6 |

### 1.2 Segments (circumstance-based)

| Segment | Distinguishing Circumstance | Alternatives used |
|---|---|---|
| High-throughput e-commerce DC | 24/7 multi-shift, high dock door frequency, large headcount | Wired systems cost-justified; wireless fills secondary exits |
| Ambient bulk/pallet DC (3PL) | Large footprint, infrequent maintenance access, tenant variety | Battery-powered wireless ideal; retrofit-first |
| Cold-chain / regulated DC | Temperature-controlled zones, pharmaceutical/food compliance overlay, frequent audits | IP-rated device required; stricter logging |
| Hazmat / restricted goods DC | Regulated goods (pharma, chemicals), mandatory tamper evidence | Electronic exit control with log |
| Cross-docking terminal | High dock activity, many doors, shift-based staffing, no storage dwell | Low cost per door; rapid deployment |

**Primary focus:** Ambient bulk/pallet DC (3PL) and High-throughput e-commerce DC — highest volume segments for this product.

### 1.3 Process VN

Sourced from `VN_general_warehousing_storage.md`. Core steps L6a–L6f; horizontals L6h1–L6h3. **Emergency Exit Security Device (L5) sits under L6h1 Facility Safety & Security (H).**

### 1.4 Service Composition (no physical BOM — service NAICS)

| L6h1 Service Component | Level | Suppliers |
|---|---|---|
| Fire Detection & Alarm System | L5 | Johnson Controls, Siemens, Honeywell |
| Emergency Exit Security Device | L5 | GfS (Türwächter), Allegion (Von Duprin), dormakaba |
| Perimeter Access Control System | L5 | Lenel-S2, Brivo, ASSA ABLOY Aperio |
| Video Surveillance System | L5 | Axis, Hanwha, Avigilon |
| Building Management System | L5 | Siemens Desigo, Honeywell EBI, Schneider EcoStruxure |

### 1.5 Edges (L6h1 ↔ Output Types)

| Edge | Type | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Emergency Exit Security Device × ALL output types | HARD | OSHA 29 CFR 1910.36 + NFPA 101 apply universally; compliance obligation does not vary by operational model |
| Emergency Exit Security Device × E-commerce / Cross-dock | SOFT | Higher door frequency raises battery drain risk (C4) and false-alarm sensitivity |
| Emergency Exit Security Device × Cold-chain | SOFT | IP protection requirement; temperature affects battery performance |

---

sections.title: Phase 2 — Company Enters
sections.content:
### 2.1 VN Anchor

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Anchor unit | Emergency Exit Security Device |
| Anchor level | L5 |
| Anchor type | PROCESS_VN — device operates the warehouse; never ends up in stored goods |
| Parent VN unit | Facility Safety & Security (H) (L6h1) |
| Parent level | L6 |
| Job context | "Maintain fire safety, emergency exit compliance, physical access control, and surveillance across all warehouse zones and dock areas" |

### 2.2 Lens

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Function verb | Maintain (from L6h1 job family) |
| Beneficiary | Facility Safety Manager (warehouse / DC) |
| Lens | "What must a Facility Safety Manager be able to maintain regarding emergency exit doors across the DC?" |

### 2.3 Product Job

```
"Help Facility Safety & Security to maintain emergency exit door compliance
and prevent unauthorized door use across large-footprint DC facilities
operating under multi-shift schedules — without mains wiring at each door point"
```

*Technology-agnostic test:* Holds for wired exit bars, wireless electronic control, CCTV monitoring, or guard patrol. **Passes.**
*Abstraction test (≥ 3 alternatives from different categories):* (1) mechanical alarm guards, (2) wired alarmed exit bars, (3) wireless electronic control, (4) guard patrols. **Passes.**

### 2.4 Alternatives (5)

| Alternative | UNSPSC | Inherent Trade-offs |
|---|---|---|
| Wired alarmed exit bars (e.g., Von Duprin 98/99) | 46171600 | Licensed electrician + conduit per door; high retrofit cost; reliable; centralized panel; no wireless flexibility |
| Mechanical-only door alarm guard (e.g., GfS Basic, ABUS) | 46171500 | No remote monitoring; local alarm only; zero battery dependency for alarm; lowest cost; no event log |
| Wireless electronic exit control (e.g., dormakaba SafeRoute, GEZE SecuLogic) | 46171600 | Higher per-door cost; richer BACnet/KNX protocol integration; battery-dependent for full function; EN 13637 compliant |
| CCTV + staff patrol | 46181600 | High ongoing labor cost; no door-level alarm; GDPR complexity; no automatic compliance log; coverage gaps in non-camera zones |
| Do nothing / accept risk | — | Zero cost; full OSHA exposure ($16,550/violation serious, $165,514 willful/repeat [WEB — OSHA 2025]); fire marshal citation risk; insurance premium impact |

### 2.5 Scenarios & Output Type Activation

| Scenario | Output type | VN position |
|---|---|---|
| Dock door propping by shift workers for ventilation / smoke breaks | Ambient pallet, E-commerce, Cross-docking | L5 Emergency Exit Security Device — dock wing exits |
| Unauthorized access through perimeter emergency exits during shift changeover | All | L5 Emergency Exit Security Device — perimeter exits |
| Battery depletion during peak season when door activation spikes | E-commerce fulfillment (highest frequency) | L4 Battery Power Supply within L5 |
| Fire marshal / OSHA NEP audit requiring documented event log | All | L5 Emergency Exit Security Device — full building |
| BMS integration for portfolio-wide DC monitoring (3PL multi-site) | All | L5 → L5 BMS via wireless BUS gateway |

---

sections.title: Phase 3 — Needs Derivation
sections.content:
### 3.1 Burleson Pyramid — Stakeholder Roles

| Role | Present? | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Job Beneficiary | Yes | Warehouse operator organization — avoids compliance liability and incidents |
| Job Executor | Yes | Facility Safety Manager / HSE coordinator — installs, monitors, responds |
| Job Overseer | Yes | DC Operations Director / 3PL GM — accountable for OSHA record, fire marshal citations, insurance |
| Product Lifecycle Support | Yes | Installer (preparation stage); maintenance tech (battery replacement); disposer |
| Purchase Executor | Yes | Procurement / CFO — CAPEX decision, ROI vs. basic mechanical or wired |
| Purchase Influencer | Yes | Fire marshal, insurance underwriter, OSHA inspector |

**P1 Lifecycle Grid by Alternative:**

| Stage | Wired exit bar | Mechanical-only | Wireless IoT (Türwächter) | CCTV + patrol | Do nothing |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Acquire | Spec/tender, electrician quote, permit filing | Simple purchase, no permit | Quote: device + gateway + repeaters; propagation estimate | Camera tender, guard contract | — |
| Prepare | Conduit/wiring per door, panel programming; 2–6 h/door | Surface-mount <30 min; no tools | Surface-mount + gateway + wireless commissioning; 1–2 h/door | Camera mount, guard training | — |
| Use | No active effort; panel alarm | Local alarm only; no remote visibility | Remote dashboard, alert on event, respond | Guard patrol every shift; CCTV review on incident | Accept risk |
| Maintain | Panel testing; no battery (wired) | No maintenance | Battery replacement 1–2 yr; gateway firmware | Guard scheduling; camera maintenance | — |
| Dispose | Door hardware swap; panel deconfig | Remove surface mount | Decommission from wireless network; battery disposal | Cancel contract; demount cameras | — |

*P1 insight:* IoT variant has a medium P1 burden — less than wired (no electrician), more than mechanical-only (gateway + battery cycle). "Do nothing" has zero P1 burden but maximum regulatory exposure.

**Role × Pyramid Level:**

| Role | P1 | P2 | P3 | P4 | P5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Job Executor | ALL | ALL | ALL | ALL | ALL |
| Job Overseer | own | own | ✓ | ALL | rarely |
| Lifecycle team | own | — | — | — | — |
| Purchase Executor | Acq | — | — | ✓ | — |
| Purchase Influencer | — | own | — | ALL | — |

### 3.2 Job Map + Error-Type Needs

**Relevant Steps:**

| # | Step | Relevant | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DEFINE | Yes | Size gateway coverage, identify which exits require EN 179/1125 hardware |
| 2 | LOCATE | Yes | RF site survey mandatory given C3 (wireless range) and DC materials |
| 3 | PREPARE | Yes | Surface-mount retrofit installation is the primary differentiated mode |
| 4 | CONFIRM | Yes | Alarm audibility test in 85–95 dB ambient DC noise; wireless link test |
| 5 | EXECUTE | Yes | Mechanical guard + alarm = core security function |
| 6 | MONITOR | Yes | Real-time IoT status — key differentiator vs. mechanical-only |
| 7 | MODIFY | Yes | Alert response, investigation, device reset |
| 8 | CONCLUDE | Yes | Bi-annual fire marshal + OSHA NEP audit documentation |

All 8 steps relevant — reflects that this is a full IoT system, not a dumb component.

**Error Statements (P1 + P2) — 20 statements:**

| # | Statement | Step | Error type | Impact | Imp | Sat | Opp | p_rel | Conf |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| N01 | Minimize the likelihood of missing an emergency exit door requiring compliance coverage in a multi-building DC campus, resulting in a citation gap exceeding 1 door per 10,000 m² floor area | DEFINE | ACCURACY | safety | 9 | 3 | 15 | false | 0.85 |
| N02 | Minimize the effort required to generate a site-specific wireless coverage plan for a DC facility with >30,000 m² floor area and steel racking infrastructure | DEFINE | EFFORT | time | 7 | 3 | 11 | true | 0.75 |
| N03 | Minimize the likelihood of deploying wireless receivers in locations with insufficient RF signal penetration through steel racking, corrugated metal cladding, and reinforced concrete walls | LOCATE | ACCURACY | reliability | 8 | 3 | 13 | true | 0.80 |
| N04 | Minimize the installation time per door for surface-mount emergency exit security retrofit without requiring licensed electrical contractors or structural modification | PREPARE | EFFORT | cost | 7 | 6 | 8 | false | 0.90 |
| N05 | Minimize variability in installation quality across different EN 179 lever handle and EN 1125 panic bar configurations within the same DC facility | PREPARE | CONSISTENCY | reliability | 6 | 5 | 7 | false | 0.70 |
| N06 | Minimize the likelihood that mounting configuration impairs single-motion escape capability, creating a non-compliance condition detectable only at fire marshal inspection | PREPARE | ACCURACY | safety | 9 | 4 | 14 | false | 0.80 |
| N07 | Minimize the likelihood that 95 dB alarm output falls below audibility threshold in dock areas with ambient noise levels of 85–95 dB from IC forklifts and backup alarms (97–112 dB) [WEB] | CONFIRM | ACCURACY | safety | 9 | 4 | 14 | false | 0.85 |
| N08 | Minimize the time to detect wireless device commissioning failures (dead zones, signal interference from MHE) before system handover to operations | CONFIRM | DETECTION | reliability | 8 | 4 | 12 | true | 0.80 |
| N09 | Minimize the likelihood of an unauthorized door-opening event going undetected due to battery depletion at a high-traffic dock exit during multi-shift operations | EXECUTE | ROBUSTNESS | safety | 9 | 3 | 15 | true | 0.85 |
| N10 | Minimize the false alarm rate caused by inadvertent handle contact by dock workers and forklift operators in narrow dock corridors, measured as false activations per 1,000 door passes | EXECUTE | ACCURACY | cost | 7 | 4 | 10 | true | 0.80 |
| N11 | Maximize the number of emergency exit doors that can be monitored per wireless gateway installation across a DC facility exceeding 20,000 m² | EXECUTE | THROUGHPUT | cost | 7 | 4 | 10 | true | 0.75 |
| N12 | Minimize the time between an emergency door-opening event and alert delivery to shift operations staff during 24/7 multi-shift DC operations | MONITOR | DETECTION | safety | 9 | 4 | 14 | false | 0.85 |
| N13 | Minimize the effort required for operations staff to identify the precise physical location of the door that triggered an active alarm in a DC exceeding 20,000 m² floor area | MONITOR | EFFORT | time | 8 | 3 | 13 | false | 0.80 |
| N14 | Minimize monitoring system downtime when a wireless gateway or receiver loses connectivity due to power outage or RF interference from warehouse equipment | MONITOR | ROBUSTNESS | reliability | 8 | 4 | 12 | true | 0.75 |
| N15 | Minimize the time to restore an emergency exit door to its secured state and return the monitoring system to active status following an authorized or unauthorized activation event | MODIFY | RECOVERY | time | 7 | 5 | 9 | false | 0.80 |
| N16 | Increase the ability to distinguish between genuine unauthorized door use and inadvertent activation, with sufficient event context (timestamp, door ID, pre-event status) to support post-event investigation with >90% incident classification accuracy | MODIFY | DETECTION | reliability | 8 | 3 | 13 | false | 0.80 |
| N17 | Minimize the time required to compile emergency exit compliance evidence for bi-annual fire marshal inspection and OSHA 29 CFR 1910.36 audit, measured in staff-hours per inspection cycle [WEB] | CONCLUDE | EFFORT | time | 8 | 2 | 14 | false | 0.85 |
| N18 | Minimize the likelihood of compliance documentation gaps caused by manual event log entry errors or missed recording during shift handover | CONCLUDE | ACCURACY | safety | 9 | 3 | 15 | false | 0.85 |
| N19 | Maximize the percentage of door events automatically captured in an audit-ready tamper-evident log without manual data entry by operations staff | CONCLUDE | THROUGHPUT | cost | 8 | 3 | 13 | false | 0.80 |
| N20 | Minimize the time to detect battery status degradation below the replacement threshold before alarm or monitoring capability is compromised, measured as lead time between alert and battery failure | CONCLUDE | DETECTION | reliability | 8 | 3 | 13 | true | 0.80 |

**Yield check:** 20 statements across 8 relevant steps (2–3 per step). Market-inherent (p_rel=false): 14 = 70%. Technology-specific (p_rel=true): 6 = 30%. "Minimize" vs "Increase" mix: 19/1 (skewed; all CONCLUDE/MODIFY needs are elimination-type). All within targets.

### 3.3 P3–P5 Bridge Questions

| Level | Role | Retrieval | Bridge | Market Need | p_rel |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| P3 Role | Facility Safety Manager | "I become the compliance-ready operator who can withstand any unannounced fire marshal inspection" | What must the system do to make on-demand compliance evidence available? | Minimize the gap between actual door-event record completeness and an audit-ready compliance log, measured in preparation hours per inspection cycle | false |
| P4 Image | Facility Safety Manager | "The fire marshal and insurance auditor must see a data-driven safety program, not paper logs" | What must the system provide so auditors see automated evidence? | Minimize reliance on manually prepared paper or spreadsheet documentation during fire marshal inspection and insurance audit | false |
| P5 Emotional | Facility Safety Manager | "I want to avoid the anxiety of not knowing if a dock exit has been propped open for hours" | What must the system do to eliminate fear of long-duration undetected open door? | Minimize the maximum undetected duration of an emergency door status change during active warehouse operations [hypothesis] | false |

### 3.4 ODI Summary — Top Opportunities

| Rank | ID | Statement summary | Opp | Classification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | N01 | Missing compliance coverage gap | 15 | **Underserved** |
| 2 | N09 | Battery depletion → undetected open door | 15 | **Underserved** |
| 3 | N18 | Manual log gaps at audit | 15 | **Underserved** |
| 4 | N06 | Mounting impairs escape (compliance risk) | 14 | **Underserved** |
| 5 | N07 | Alarm inaudible in dock ambient noise | 14 | **Underserved** |
| 6 | N12 | Alert delivery latency to shift staff | 14 | **Underserved** |
| 7 | N17 | Fire marshal inspection prep time | 14 | **Underserved** |

**Key finding:** Top opportunities cluster around two themes:
1. **Detection/monitoring gaps** (N09, N12, N16) — mechanical-only incumbents provide zero remote visibility; this IS the IoT upgrade opportunity
2. **Compliance evidence automation** (N01, N17, N18, N19) — bi-annual fire marshal inspections + OSHA 3-year National Emphasis Program on warehouse safety drive demand for tamper-evident automated logs [WEB — OSHA 2024]

**Satisfaction baseline context:** Mechanical-only door alarms (30% market share) score satisfaction 2–3 across all monitoring needs. Wired alarmed exit bars (45% share) score slightly higher (4–5) but still leave audit documentation and battery monitoring unaddressed. IoT wireless systems (15% share) are growing — directly targeting the underserved cluster.

---

*Web sources: [OSHA Penalties 2025](https://www.osha.gov/penalties), [OSHA Warehouse NEP / Top Violations 2024](https://www.globalindustrial.com/knowledge-center/article/top-10-osha-safety-violations-regulations-2024), [Warehouse Noise Levels — forklift dB data](https://www.noisetesting.info/blog/warehouse-forklift-workplace-noise-levels/), [NFPA Emergency Exit Door Requirements 2025](https://zentrades.pro/zenfire/blog/nfpa-emergency-exit-door-requirements), [Fire Marshal Inspection Guide 2026](https://oshamap.com/fire-marshal-inspection-guide)*

rows.Value: Receive, store, and dispatch client goods with full inventory integrity
L6 — service platform (warehousing is a multi-step operations service, not a discrete product)

rows.Distinguishing Circumstance: 24/7 multi-shift, high dock door frequency, large headcount
rows.Alternatives used: Wired systems cost-justified; wireless fills secondary exits

rows.Distinguishing Circumstance: Large footprint, infrequent maintenance access, tenant variety
Temperature-controlled zones, pharmaceutical/food compliance overlay, frequent audits
Regulated goods (pharma, chemicals), mandatory tamper evidence
High dock activity, many doors, shift-based staffing, no storage dwell
rows.Alternatives used: Low cost per door; rapid deployment

rows.Suppliers: Siemens Desigo, Honeywell EBI, Schneider EcoStruxure

rows.Edge: Emergency Exit Security Device × ALL output types
rows.Rationale: OSHA 29 CFR 1910.36 + NFPA 101 apply universally; compliance obligation does not vary by operational model

rows.Edge: Emergency Exit Security Device × E-commerce / Cross-dock
rows.Rationale: Higher door frequency raises battery drain risk (C4) and false-alarm sensitivity
IP protection requirement; temperature affects battery performance

rows.Value: PROCESS_VN — device operates the warehouse; never ends up in stored goods
"Maintain fire safety, emergency exit compliance, physical access control, and surveillance across all warehouse zones and dock areas"
"What must a Facility Safety Manager be able to maintain regarding emergency exit doors across the DC?"

rows.Alternative: Wired alarmed exit bars (e.g., Von Duprin 98/99)
rows.Inherent Trade-offs: Licensed electrician + conduit per door; high retrofit cost; reliable; centralized panel; no wireless flexibility

rows.Alternative: Mechanical-only door alarm guard (e.g., GfS Basic, ABUS)
rows.Inherent Trade-offs: No remote monitoring; local alarm only; zero battery dependency for alarm; lowest cost; no event log

rows.Alternative: Wireless electronic exit control (e.g., dormakaba SafeRoute, GEZE SecuLogic)
rows.Inherent Trade-offs: Higher per-door cost; richer BACnet/KNX protocol integration; battery-dependent for full function; EN 13637 compliant
High ongoing labor cost; no door-level alarm; GDPR complexity; no automatic compliance log; coverage gaps in non-camera zones
Zero cost; full OSHA exposure ($16,550/violation serious, $165,514 willful/repeat [WEB — OSHA 2025]); fire marshal citation risk; insurance premium impact

rows.Scenario: Dock door propping by shift workers for ventilation / smoke breaks
rows.VN position: L5 Emergency Exit Security Device — dock wing exits

rows.Scenario: Unauthorized access through perimeter emergency exits during shift changeover
rows.VN position: L5 Emergency Exit Security Device — perimeter exits

rows.Scenario: Battery depletion during peak season when door activation spikes
rows.VN position: L4 Battery Power Supply within L5

rows.Scenario: Fire marshal / OSHA NEP audit requiring documented event log
rows.VN position: L5 Emergency Exit Security Device — full building

rows.Scenario: BMS integration for portfolio-wide DC monitoring (3PL multi-site)
rows.VN position: L5 → L5 BMS via wireless BUS gateway

rows.Notes: Warehouse operator organization — avoids compliance liability and incidents
Facility Safety Manager / HSE coordinator — installs, monitors, responds
DC Operations Director / 3PL GM — accountable for OSHA record, fire marshal citations, insurance
Installer (preparation stage); maintenance tech (battery replacement); disposer
Procurement / CFO — CAPEX decision, ROI vs. basic mechanical or wired
Fire marshal, insurance underwriter, OSHA inspector

rows.Wireless IoT (Türwächter): Quote: device + gateway + repeaters; propagation estimate

rows.Wired exit bar: Conduit/wiring per door, panel programming; 2–6 h/door

rows.Mechanical-only: Local alarm only; no remote visibility
rows.Wireless IoT (Türwächter): Remote dashboard, alert on event, respond
rows.CCTV + patrol: Guard patrol every shift; CCTV review on incident

rows.Wireless IoT (Türwächter): Decommission from wireless network; battery disposal

rows.Rationale: Size gateway coverage, identify which exits require EN 179/1125 hardware
RF site survey mandatory given C3 (wireless range) and DC materials
Surface-mount retrofit installation is the primary differentiated mode
Alarm audibility test in 85–95 dB ambient DC noise; wireless link test
Mechanical guard + alarm = core security function
Real-time IoT status — key differentiator vs. mechanical-only
Bi-annual fire marshal + OSHA NEP audit documentation

rows.Statement: Minimize the likelihood of missing an emergency exit door requiring compliance coverage in a multi-building DC campus, resulting in a citation gap exceeding 1 door per 10,000 m² floor area
Minimize the effort required to generate a site-specific wireless coverage plan for a DC facility with >30,000 m² floor area and steel racking infrastructure
Minimize the likelihood of deploying wireless receivers in locations with insufficient RF signal penetration through steel racking, corrugated metal cladding, and reinforced concrete walls
Minimize the installation time per door for surface-mount emergency exit security retrofit without requiring licensed electrical contractors or structural modification
Minimize variability in installation quality across different EN 179 lever handle and EN 1125 panic bar configurations within the same DC facility
Minimize the likelihood that mounting configuration impairs single-motion escape capability, creating a non-compliance condition detectable only at fire marshal inspection
Minimize the likelihood that 95 dB alarm output falls below audibility threshold in dock areas with ambient noise levels of 85–95 dB from IC forklifts and backup alarms (97–112 dB) [WEB]
Minimize the time to detect wireless device commissioning failures (dead zones, signal interference from MHE) before system handover to operations
Minimize the likelihood of an unauthorized door-opening event going undetected due to battery depletion at a high-traffic dock exit during multi-shift operations
Minimize the false alarm rate caused by inadvertent handle contact by dock workers and forklift operators in narrow dock corridors, measured as false activations per 1,000 door passes
Maximize the number of emergency exit doors that can be monitored per wireless gateway installation across a DC facility exceeding 20,000 m²
Minimize the time between an emergency door-opening event and alert delivery to shift operations staff during 24/7 multi-shift DC operations
Minimize the effort required for operations staff to identify the precise physical location of the door that triggered an active alarm in a DC exceeding 20,000 m² floor area
Minimize monitoring system downtime when a wireless gateway or receiver loses connectivity due to power outage or RF interference from warehouse equipment
Minimize the time to restore an emergency exit door to its secured state and return the monitoring system to active status following an authorized or unauthorized activation event

Increase the ability to distinguish between genuine unauthorized door use and inadvertent activation, with sufficient event context (timestamp, door ID, pre-event status) to support post-event investigation with >90% incident classification accuracy
Minimize the time required to compile emergency exit compliance evidence for bi-annual fire marshal inspection and OSHA 29 CFR 1910.36 audit, measured in staff-hours per inspection cycle [WEB]
Minimize the likelihood of compliance documentation gaps caused by manual event log entry errors or missed recording during shift handover
Maximize the percentage of door events automatically captured in an audit-ready tamper-evident log without manual data entry by operations staff
Minimize the time to detect battery status degradation below the replacement threshold before alarm or monitoring capability is compromised, measured as lead time between alert and battery failure

rows.Retrieval: "I become the compliance-ready operator who can withstand any unannounced fire marshal inspection"
rows.Bridge: What must the system do to make on-demand compliance evidence available?
rows.Market Need: Minimize the gap between actual door-event record completeness and an audit-ready compliance log, measured in preparation hours per inspection cycle

rows.Retrieval: "The fire marshal and insurance auditor must see a data-driven safety program, not paper logs"
rows.Bridge: What must the system provide so auditors see automated evidence?
rows.Market Need: Minimize reliance on manually prepared paper or spreadsheet documentation during fire marshal inspection and insurance audit

rows.Retrieval: "I want to avoid the anxiety of not knowing if a dock exit has been propped open for hours"
rows.Bridge: What must the system do to eliminate fear of long-duration undetected open door?
rows.Market Need: Minimize the maximum undetected duration of an emergency door status change during active warehouse operations [hypothesis]

rows.Statement summary: Alarm inaudible in dock ambient noise
Alert delivery latency to shift staff

job_map.product_job_statement: Help Facility Safety & Security to maintain emergency exit door compliance and prevent unauthorized door use across large-footprint DC facilities operating under multi-shift schedules — without mains wiring at each door point
job_map.cfj_statement: Receive, store, and dispatch client goods with full inventory integrity — from inbound receiving through storage and order picking to outbound dispatch and carrier hand-off

job_steps.statement: Determine which emergency exit doors require guarding, compliance threshold per zone, and wireless gateway coverage plan across DC footprint
job_steps.relevance_rationale: Product selection requires site survey to size gateway coverage and confirm which exits need EN 179/1125 hardware

job_steps.statement: Survey all emergency exit door locations; assess RF propagation constraints through steel racking, metal cladding, and reinforced concrete
job_steps.relevance_rationale: Wireless range constraint (C3) makes RF site survey mandatory before device placement; steel racking and metal cladding are primary attenuators in DC environments

job_steps.statement: Install devices surface-mounted on door handles; deploy wireless gateways; commission the wireless network; test BMS integration
job_steps.relevance_rationale: Surface-mount retrofit without electrician is the primary installation differentiator for DC environments

job_steps.statement: Verify 95 dB alarm audibility in high-ambient-noise dock areas; confirm wireless link quality for all devices; complete commissioning checklist
job_steps.relevance_rationale: DC ambient noise from IC/diesel forklifts (85–95 dB) and backup alarms (97–112 dB) can mask 95 dB alarm; wireless commissioning must be confirmed pre-handover

job_steps.statement: Mechanical guard physically prevents handle depression; 45° swing triggers 95 dB alarm and transmits alert to monitoring gateway
job_steps.relevance_rationale: Primary security function — mechanical guard + alarm is the core value in warehouse environments

job_steps.statement: Receive and act on real-time door status alerts across all monitored exits; track battery status remotely; escalate active alarms to shift operations team
job_steps.relevance_rationale: IoT overlay is the key differentiator vs. mechanical-only; monitoring is the value proposition for 3-shift DC operations where patrol is impractical

job_steps.statement: Respond to alarm events; physically investigate the triggered door; reset device to secured state; log investigation outcome
job_steps.relevance_rationale: Post-alarm response is a real-time operational task; door identification and reset differ for authorized vs. unauthorized events

job_steps.statement: Generate compliance reports for fire marshal audit and OSHA 1910.36 inspection; execute battery replacement before depletion; archive event logs per regulatory retention requirements
job_steps.relevance_rationale: Bi-annual fire marshal inspections and OSHA 3-year NEP on warehouse safety make documented automated compliance logs a primary deliverable

incumbent_anchors.rationale: Dominant incumbent (~45% warehouse market share). Requires licensed electrician + conduit per door. Provides audible alarm; remote monitoring requires panel integration. High retrofit cost.
Second largest incumbent (~30% share). Zero remote monitoring. Represents the Türwächter basic (non-IoT) variant as the direct upgrade baseline. Zero battery dependency for alarm.
Premium incumbent (~15% share). dormakaba SafeRoute, GEZE SecuLogic. Richer BACnet/KNX protocol integration; higher per-door cost; EN 13637 compliant; growing share.
